FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study: 10,574 early adolescents aged 9-14 from baseline 2016 to January 2021

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with cross-sectional data at T1 (baseline 9-11y) and T2 (~2 years later 11-14y) and longitudinal linear mixed models

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original research article published in Journal of Adolescent Health 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as composite "≤2 hours of screen time" within 24-hour movement guidelines bundle without per-device/per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM; classified as multi-component movement behavior (sleep + screen time + physical activity) without isolation of smartphone or social media use

notes: Screen time operationalized as adherence guideline without device/platform breakdown; no separate phone or SM variable

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study; multi-center prospective cohort study enrolling 11,875 children aged 9-10 from 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with cross-sectional analysis at year 2 and longitudinal analysis from year 2 to year 3 using multivariable logistic regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original research article published in Drug and Alcohol Dependence 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary IV is cyberbullying victimization (not smartphone or social media use). No separately-measured smartphone or SM variable appears as IV or DV. Per v6 criteria: "Cyberbullying alone (without a separately-measured smartphone or SM variable) does NOT qualify"

notes: Cyberbullying is construct measured, not smartphone/SM use; substance use initiation is outcome, not mediator or covariate

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; 5,027 adolescents assessed before pandemic (10-13 years) and across six timepoints May 2020-March 2021

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using mixed-effect models to examine longitudinal associations between self-reported sleep and screen time across pandemic timepoints

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original research article published in Health Psychology 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Recreational screen time operationalized as separate constructs: social media use and video gaming (not composite). Methods state questions assessed "social media use" and "playing single player, and video games" separately. Sleep (time in bed, bedtimes, sleep onset latency) is individual-trait-level DV. Paper states: "Higher social media use and video gaming were associated with shorter time in bed, later bedtimes, and longer sleep onset latency"

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effect models report numeric results for SM (IV) ↔ sleep outcome (DV) associations with statistical significance; results show associations between screen time variables and sleep measures with p-values and effect estimates reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results reported throughout: time in bed varied with assessment (χ²(6)=1987.61, p<.01); social media use and video gaming were significantly associated with sleep outcomes; detailed slope analyses provided for bedtime and sleep onset latency relationships

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal; however SM (social media, video gaming) functions as IV predicting sleep outcomes, not exclusively as DV. EC7 does not apply when SM is used as IV in any analysis
notes: Longitudinal design with SM operationalized as separate (social media, video gaming) IVs predicting sleep outcomes; meets all inclusion criteria
